---
title: "Noise"
format: pdf
---

# General Question
Using the matrix-variate normal distribution can we define a noise component similar to that of the Banfield and Raftery noise, or the Hennig and Coretto. 

# Matrix-Variate Normal Distribution

- n is the number of subjects
- r is the number of occasions (time points)
- p is the number of attributes

$$
\phi_{r\times c}(\mathbf{X} | \mathbf{M}, \mathbf{V}, \mathbf{U}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{\frac{rc}{2}}|\mathbf{V}|^{\frac{r}{2}}|\mathbf{U}|^{\frac{p}{2}}} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}\text{tr}\left(\mathbf{V}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{M})^\top\mathbf{U}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{M})\right) \right\}
$$

- M is a r x c mean matrix
- U is a r x r column covariance matrix
- V is a c x c column row covariance matrix

Note 1: that there is an identifiability issue with regard to U and V. If $k$ is a strictly positive constant, that replacing U and V by $(1/k)\mathbf{U}$ and $k\mathbf{V}$ respectively, leaves the distribution unchanged. Solutions have been proposed including setting $\text{tr}(\mathbf{U}) = r$ or $\mathbf{U}_{11} = 1$.

$$
\frac{1}{k}\mathbf{V} \otimes k\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{V} \otimes \mathbf{U}, \qquad \text{if} \ k >0
$$

Note 2: that the matrix-variate normal distribution is related to the multivariate normal distribution via the equivalence

$$
\mathbf{X} \sim \mathcal{N}_{r\times c} (\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{U}, \mathbf{V}) \iff \text{vec}({\mathbf{X}}) \sim \mathcal{N}_{rc}(\text{vec}(\mathbf{M}), \mathbf{V} \otimes \mathbf{U})
$$

## EM Updates

Let $z_{ig} = 1$ when observation $i$ belongs in group $g$, and $z_{ig} = 0$ else. Then, in the E-step we estimate the posterior $\hat{z}_{ig}$ that observation $\mathbf{X}_i$ belongs to group $g$

$$
\hat{z}_{ig} = \frac{\hat\pi_g\phi_{r\times c}\left(\mathbf{X}_i|\hat{\mathbf{M}}_g,\hat{\mathbf{V}}_g, \hat{\mathbf{U}}_g\right)}{\sum_{h=1}^G\pi_h\phi_{r\times c}\left(\mathbf{X}_i|\hat{\mathbf{M}}_h, \hat{\mathbf{V}}_h, \hat{\mathbf{U}}_h\right)}
$$

In M-step we get the maximum likelihood estimates:

$$
\begin{align}

\hat\pi_g &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig} \\

\hat{\mathbf{M}}_g &= \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}}\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}\mathbf{X}_i \\

\hat{\mathbf{U}}_g &= \frac{1}{p\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}}\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}}_g)\hat{\mathbf{V}}_g^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}}_g)^\top \\

\hat{\mathbf{V}}_g &= \frac{1}{r\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}}\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}}_g)^\top\hat{\mathbf{U}}_g^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}}_g)

\end{align}
$$

We can initialize $\hat{z}_{ig}$ using k-means by vectorizing the matrices first. 

Meaning we define the Mahalonobis distance as

$$
\mathcal{D}_M(\mathbf{X}_i, \mathbf{M}, \mathbf{V}, \mathbf{U}) = \text{tr}\left\{\mathbf{U}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{M})\mathbf{V^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{M})^\top} \right\}
$$

These will form as the foundation for our base Matrix Gaussian Mixture Model (MGMM) updates. Let us try putting that into a function!

## Noise

### Banfield and Raftery

BR-noise augments the GMM with a uniform noise component supported on the convex hull of the data. For univariate data, this simplifies to a uniform distribution over the observed data range:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{g = 1}^{G} \pi_g \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \vert \boldsymbol\mu_g, \boldsymbol\Sigma_g) + \pi_0\frac{1(x\in[x_{\min},x_{\max}])}{x_{\max}-x_{\min}}, \tag{1.1} \\
\sum_{g=0}^G\pi_g = 1, \quad \pi_0 \geq 0
$$ 
where the mixture weights satisfy $\sum_{g=0}^G \pi_g = 1$ and $\pi_0 \ge 0$.

The general multivariate formulation extends this idea to higher dimensions, $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ are replaced the convex hull of the dataset, and the uniform component’s density is constant within that region.

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{g = 1}^{G} \pi_g \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \vert \boldsymbol\mu_g, \boldsymbol\Sigma_g) + \pi_0 \ \mathcal{U}(\mathbf{x}) \tag{1.2}
$$

### Improper-k

HC-Noise replaces the bounded uniform density with a constant improper density $k$ defined over the entire space $\mathbb{R}^p$.

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{g = 1}^{G} \pi_g \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \vert \boldsymbol\mu_g, \boldsymbol\Sigma_g) + \pi_0 k \tag{1.3}
$$

Here $k$ is improper and thus is not a proper probability density since it does not integrate to 1. However, it still defines a pseudo-likelihood; 
$$
\tilde L(\theta)=\prod_{i=1}^n\left[\sum_{g=1}^G\pi_g\phi(\mathbf x_i|\boldsymbol{\mu_g}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma_g}) + \pi_0k \right]
$$ 
whose maximization will yield robust parameter estimates. Again, the background term $k$ acts as a "catch-all" component that absorbs observations poorly explained by any Gaussian cluster.

## Matrix Noise

For matrix-valued observations, the noise term should play the same role as the Banfield-Raftery or Hennig-Coretto background component: it should absorb matrices that are not well explained by any Gaussian cluster, without forcing them to belong to a particular structured component.

$$
f(\mathbf{X}) = \sum_{g=1}^{G} \pi_g \, \phi_{r\times c}(\mathbf{X} \,|\, \mathbf{M}_g, \mathbf{U}_g, \mathbf{V}_g) + \pi_0 \, h(\mathbf{X}),
\qquad \sum_{g=0}^{G} \pi_g = 1
$$

where $h(\mathbf{X})$ is a noise density and $\pi_0$ is the noise proportion.

### Improper k matrix-HC-noise

Let us define our improper noise $k$ as

$$
h(\mathbf{X}) = k, \qquad k > 0 \quad \forall \ \mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{rc}
$$

This is again going to be a pseudo-likelihood as $k$ will not integrate to 1 over the support $\mathbb{R}^{rc}$. 
Then the mixture becomes
$$
f(\mathbf{X}) = \sum_{g=1}^{G} \pi_g \, \phi_{r\times c}(\mathbf{X} \,|\, \mathbf{M}_g, \mathbf{U}_g, \mathbf{V}_g) + \pi_0 k
$$

Thus, the noise responsibility becomes
$$
\hat z_{i0} = \frac{\hat\pi_0 h(\mathbf{X}_i)}{\hat\pi_0 k + \sum_{g=1}^{G} \hat\pi_g \phi_{r\times c}(\mathbf{X}_i \,|\, \hat{\mathbf{M}}_g, \hat{\mathbf{U}}_g, \hat{\mathbf{V}}_g)}
$$

To pick $k$ we can automate a grid search over candidate values and choose the one with the best matrix-KS goodness-of-fit score for the fitted distances.

### Uniform BR-noise

For BR-noise, consider vectorizing the matrices and then computing the convex hull over the $\mathbb{R}^{rc}$ dimensional space. 

The uniform density is then defined as
$$
\mathcal{U}(\mathbf{X}) = \frac{1}{V}
$$

where $V$ is the volume of the convex hull. This will probably work fine in low dimensions, but as dimensions increase the volume of the convex hull will also increase. Then blowing up the spatial density resulting in mini densities. Will have to test and keep an eye out for that. 

## Kolmogorov-Smirnov Goodness of Fit Test
 
The KS test determines whether a sample comes from a specified distribution. Motivating the test in terms of MSDs, we define the test where the distances compose a sample from the reference distribution. The empirical distribution function for IID. ordered $\mathcal{D}_1,\dots,\mathcal{D}_N$ is given by

$$
F_N(D) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N\mathbb{I}_{(-\infty,D]}(\mathcal{D}_i) \\
\text{where} \\
\mathbb{I}_{(-\infty,D]}= \begin{cases}1 & \text{if} \ \mathcal{D_i\leq D,} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The KS statistic for a given cumulative distribution function $F(D)$ is

$$
\mathbb{D}_N = \underset{D}{\text{sup}}|F_N(D) - F(D)|
$$

This test statistic can be modified to account for two samples, because we compare distances from two estimates which theoretically converge to the same distribution. The two-sample KS test statistic is

$$
\mathbb{D}_{N_a, N_b} = \underset{D}{\text{sup}}|F_{1,N_a}(D) - F_{2,N_b}(D)|
$$
where $N_a$ and $N_b$ are the numbers of observations in samples $a$ and $b$. $F_{1,N_a}$ and $F_{2,N_b}$ denote the cumulative distribution functions defined for the first and second samples. Our null is that both samples come from the same distribution and our alternative is that they do not.
Null is rejected at signficance level $\alpha$ if

$$
\mathbb{D}_{N_a, N_b} > \sqrt{-\frac{1}{2}\log(\alpha)\left(\frac{N_a + N_b}{N_aN_b}\right)}
$$
This test could be used to test matrix variate normality by comparing the distributions of the two distances. The main benefit is that it does not specify the common distribution between two samples. However, it is not as powerful as the original KS test because it is sensitive against all possible types of differences between two distribution functions. 

## Distance-Distance Plot

The DD plot is a scatter plot of the Mahalanobis distances using the estimated parameters from the multivariate and matrix variate normal distribution, respectively. Consider $N$ $r \times c$ matrices $\mathbf{X}_1,\dots \mathbf{X}_N$ such that each $\mathbf{X}_i$ is drawn from a matrix variate random variable $\mathcal{X} \sim \mathcal{N}_{r\times c}(\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{V}, \mathbf{U})$. Let $\mu = \text{vec}(\mathbf{M})$ and $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{V} \otimes \mathbf{U}$. Consider the estimates

$$
\hat\mu = \sum_{i=1}^N\frac{\text{vec}(\mathbf{X_i})}{N} \qquad
\text{and} \qquad
\hat\Sigma = \frac{1}{N - 1}\sum_{i=1}^N\{\text{vec}(\mathbf{X_i}) - \hat\mu \}\{\text{vec}(\mathbf{X_i})-\hat\mu\}^\top
$$
 
Suppose we calculate the MSD for each observation $\mathbf{X}_i$ in a give sample as follows:
$$
\mathcal{D}(\mathbf{X}_i, \hat\mu, \hat\Sigma) = \{\text{vec}(\mathbf{X_i}) - \hat\mu \}^\top\hat\Sigma\{\text{vec}(\mathbf{X_i})-\hat\mu\}
$$
 
We have that
$$
\frac{N}{(N-1)^2}\mathcal{D}(\mathbf{X_i}, \hat\mu, \hat\Sigma) \sim \text{Beta}\left(\frac{rc}{2},\frac{N-rc-1}{2}\right)
$$
Note: It is not realistic to use a beta distribution when calculating the KS scores for the automatic k. Instead I use a chi-square distribution, as when the beta tends to infinity it is a chi-square. I could also use a gamma.

The maximum likelihood estimates for $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{U}, \mathbf{V}$ are

$$
\begin{align}
\hat{\mathbf{M}} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N\mathbf{X}_i \\

\hat{\mathbf{U}} &= \frac{1}{cN}\sum_{i=1}^N(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}})\hat{\mathbf{V}}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}})^\top \\

\hat{\mathbf{V}} &= \frac{1}{rN}\sum_{i=1}^N(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}})^\top\hat{\mathbf{U}}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}})
\end{align}
$$

Now let
$$
\mathcal{D}_M(\mathbf{X}_i, \mathbf{M}, \mathbf{V}, \mathbf{U}) = \text{tr}\{\mathbf{U}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i-\mathbf{M})\mathbf{V}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i-\mathbf{M})^\top\}
$$

This quantity in (11) can be viewed as the matrix variate version of the MSD, and we will use this terminology when referring to this quantity. Then if a Kronecker product structure exists for $\Sigma$, then:

$$
\mathcal{D}(\mathbf{X}_i, \mu, \Sigma) = \mathcal{D}_M(\mathbf{X}_i, \mathbf{M}, \mathbf{V}, \mathbf{U}) \\
\mathcal{D}(\mathbf{X}_i, \mathbf{\hat M}, \mathbf{\hat V}, \mathbf{\hat U}) \to \mathcal{D}_M(\mathbf{X}_i, \mathbf{M}, \mathbf{V}, \mathbf{U}) \\

\mathcal{D}(\mathbf{X}_i, \hat\mu, \hat\Sigma) \to \mathcal{D}_M(\mathbf{X}_i, \mu, \Sigma)
$$

From this, it seems useful to visualize matrix variate normality by comparing the estimated MSDs. Consider a plot of $\mathcal{D}$ versus $\mathcal{D}_M$ , which we will refer to as the distance-distance ($DD$) plot. The $DD$ plot is a scatter plot of the Mahalanobis distances using the estimated parameters from the multivariate and matrix variate normal distribution, respectively.

## Test for Matrix Variate Normality

Once the distances have all been calculated, we perform the KS test at signifcance level $\alpha$. The test for matrix variate normality is defined as

$$
\begin{align}
H_0 : \mathcal{D} \ \text{and} \ \mathcal{D}_M \ &\text{come from the same distribution}. \\
H_a : \mathcal{D} \ \text{and} \ \mathcal{D}_M \ &\text{do not come from the same distribution}. \\
\end{align}
$$

Null is rejected if

$$
\mathbb{D}_{N, N} > \sqrt{-\frac{1}{2}\log(\alpha)\left(\frac{2N}{N^2}\right)}
$$
Because both distances are estimated from the same sample, they must theoretically converge to the same distribution. Rejection of the null hypothesis indicates that a Kronecker product covariance structure is not present.
Our distance measure in all of these is the matrix version of MSD the Manahalobis distance defined above. 

## In the Style of Viroli

A total of 100 datasets are created with n = 300 $3 \times 5$ matrices in three groups with proportions $\pi = (0.3, 0.4, 0.3)$ and means of the form

$$
M_g = \begin{pmatrix}
      a & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0\\
      b & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0\\
      0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0
      \end{pmatrix}
$$

with M_1 : $(a,b)$ = $(0.5, 0.5)$, M_2 : $(a,b)$ = $(0,0)$ and M_3 : $(a,b)$ = $(-0.5, 0.5)$. U_g and V_g matrices of dimensions $3\times 3 \ \text{and} \ 5\times 5$ are randomly generated using the rcorrmatrix function in the clusterGeneration package. Where our simulation differs is in the generation of outliers. To simulate potential outliers due to errors in data-entry, 15 matrices are randomly selected and the entries in each matrix are randomly permuted.

## In the style of Tomarchio

A total of 100 datasets are created with n = 200 $2 \times 4$ matrices in two groups of equal weight with parameters

$$
\begin{align}
M_1 = \begin{pmatrix}
        −2.60 & -1.10 & -0.50 & -0.20\\
        1.30 & 0.60 & 0.30 & 0.10
      \end{pmatrix}
\qquad
&M_2 = \begin{pmatrix}
        1.50 & 1.70 & 1.90 & 2.20\\
        -3.70 & -2.70 & -2.00 & -1.50
      \end{pmatrix} \\
      
U_1 = \begin{pmatrix}
        2 & 0 \\
        0 & 1
      \end{pmatrix}
\qquad
&U_2 = \begin{pmatrix}
        1.70 & 0.5 \\
        0.5 & 1.30
      \end{pmatrix}
\end{align}     
\\
      
V_1 = V_2 = \begin{pmatrix}
        1.00 & 0.50 & 0.25 & 0.13 \\
        0.50 & 1.00 & 0.50 & 0.25 \\
        0.25 & 0.50 & 1.00 & 0.50 \\
        0.13 & 0.25 & 0.50 & 1.00
      \end{pmatrix} \\
$$
Ten matrices are randomly selected, and then one column from each selected matrix is randomly replaced with data generated from Unif(−15, 15).
